title: Time crystals in cavity-BEC systems
abstract: The understanding of light-induced dynamical states continues to be a challenging and fruitful pursuit of science. This pursuit is supported by quantum simulation of dynamical phenomena, e.g., in ultracold atom systems. Typically, ultracold atom dynamics are read out destructively, via time-of-flight imaging, limiting a detailed analysis. However, atom-cavity systems provide a real-time readout of the photonic state via photon emission from the cavity, making the system ideally suited for the simulation of dynamical phenomena. Here, we review three distinct time crystalline states, predicted and realized in a cavity-BEC system. We give an example for each of them, based on minimal few-mode models. We characterize the time crystalline states via correlation functions of the cavity mode, and characteristic momentum modes of the condensate. This supports a clear distinction between these time crystals. More generally, the sequence of studies reviewed here, serves as a blueprint for setting up minimal models and their characterization, for dynamical phenomena.
summary: This review paper explores the emergence of different types of time crystals in a Bose-Einstein Condensate coupled to an optical cavity. It demonstrates how varying the driving protocol (intensity, phase, or constant driving) can induce commensurate, incommensurate, or continuous time crystalline phases. The work highlights the utility of atom-cavity systems as powerful platforms for simulating complex dynamical phenomena.
main_problem: The study aims to identify, characterize, and classify three distinct types of time crystalline states (commensurate, incommensurate, and continuous) emerging in driven-dissipative cavity-BEC systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that cavity-BEC systems can host various time crystals, ranging from period-doubling states induced by intensity modulation to continuous time crystals occurring under constant driving, and show that long-range order is achieved in the thermodynamic limit.
method: The study uses semiclassical dynamics via Heisenberg-Langevin equations, numerical integration with the Heun algorithm, and quantum-classical rescaling to investigate the thermodynamic limit.
model_or_system: The system consists of a Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) inside a high-finesse optical cavity under transverse pumping, which can be mapped onto the open Dicke model using two-mode or three-mode expansions.
key_observables: Cavity mode photon correlation functions (c_ph), checkerboard density wave correlation functions (c_dw), and bond density wave correlation functions (c_bdw).
important_parameters: Pump intensity, cavity detuning, photon decay rate (kappa), and the atom number (N_a) used for scaling to the thermodynamic limit.
main_assumptions: The system is treated in the dispersive regime where atoms occupy a single internal state, and the dynamics are approximated using a few-mode expansion (two-mode or three-mode).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the physical regimes of the pump field, the momentum-space distribution of atoms, the driving protocols for different time crystals, trajectories of the cavity field, and parametric representations of correlation functions in the complex plane.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the cavity-BEC platform and the Dicke transition, then progresses through the review of three specific time crystalline states (commensurate, incommensurate, and continuous) using increasing model complexity, and concludes with a summary of the characterization framework and the thermodynamic limit.
why_interesting: It provides a blueprint for using cavity-BEC systems as versatile quantum simulators for complex non-equilibrium phenomena and explores the emergence of exotic time-translation symmetry breaking in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. As the platform for creating time crystals, we consider a cavity-BEC system that is pumped from the transverse direction. For weak pump field, panel (a), the standing field of the retroreflected pump beam creates a weak optical lattice. In momentum space, shown in the lower panel, two side peaks are generated at ±2kez, in addition to the condensate peak at k = 0. The cavity is essentially silent. For a strong pump field, the system forms a superradiant state. The atoms form a checkerboard lattice, generating additional side peaks at ±key ± kez, shown in the lower panel. The cavity mode is activated, leading to photoemission through the semitransparent mirror. The transition from the...

FIG. 2. We discuss three time crystalline states, emerging in a cavity-BEC system. By modulating the intensity of the pump beam, we induce a commensurate, discrete time crystal. By modulating the phase of the pump beam, we induce an incommensurate time crystal. For continuous driving, we induce a continuous time crystal. These time crystalline states are defined via the time-dependent correlation functions of the cavity mode and specific atomic modes, as we discuss in this review.

FIG. 3. To illustrate the dynamical evolution in terms of trajectories, we show the linear ramp into the Dicke ordered state. In panel (a) we show the pump protocol, which is linear up to a time tramp = 18 ms, and constant after that at ϵp(t) = ϵf = 3.3. In panel (b) we show several trajectories αr(t), generated by the Heisenberg-Langevin equations Eqs. 9 –11. They display dynamical Z2 symmetry breaking, and then settle in one of the two ordered states.

FIG. 4. In panel (a) we show the real part of the correlation function of the photon mode cph(t) for the ordered (blue) and disordered state (red). The ordered state has been generated with ϵf = 3.3, based on the protocol shown in Fig. 3 (a), and the disordered state has been generated with ϵf = 0.3. In panel (b) we show the correlation function of the checker- board density order cdw(t) for the ordered (blue) and disor- dered state (red). The fast decay to zero for the disordered state, or to a non-zero value for long times, for the ordered state, indicates temporal short-range or long-range order, re- spectively.

FIG. 5. In panel (a) we display the ramp and driving protocol. The intensity is first ramped up to a value ϵf = 3.3 in the superradiant state. After that, the intensity is modulated, with a relative amplitude f0 = 0.28, and a driving frequency ωdr = 2π × 10 kHz. In panel (b) we display a trajectory of the real part of the photon field. After the intensity driving is turned on, the system first displays a transient regime, and then settles into a discrete time crystalline state, which is commensurate to the driving frequency. Specifically it is a period-doubling state.
